# **Chapter 4** {#chapter-4}

# **Implementation and Testing** {#implementation-and-testing}

## **4.1 Algorithm Design** {#4.1-algorithm-design}

This chapter describes the algorithmic design of the core modules implemented in this iteration, specifically: **Knowledge Graph Construction**, **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)**, **Enhanced Transcription Pipeline** improvements, **Short-Form Content Generation**, and **Expert Review through Flagging and Metrics**. Each module outlines the underlying logic, processing flow, and pseudocode used to implement the system. The emphasis is on why each workflow was needed, how it supports verified Islamic content delivery, and how the implementation improves reliability, usability, and quality assurance.

### **4.1.1 Enhanced Transcription Pipeline (RabbitMQ Queue \+ Worker-Based Processing)** {#4.1.1-enhanced-transcription-pipeline-(rabbitmq-queue-+-worker-based-processing)}

![][image10]

To achieve a scalable, fault-tolerant, and decoupled transcription workflow, the transcription subsystem was architected around a **message-queue–based pipeline** using **RabbitMQ** and dedicated worker nodes. This redesign ensures continuous processing, resilience to failures, and support for parallel execution as the dataset grows.

The pipeline consists of two coordinated stages: (1) transcription using Whisper, and (2) optional LLM-based correction using Gemini. Both stages are orchestrated via persistent RabbitMQ queues to ensure at-least-once delivery, retry-based fault recovery, and horizontal scalability.

#### **A. Transcription Queue (RabbitMQ Persistent Queue)** {#a.-transcription-queue-(rabbitmq-persistent-queue)}

When an audio lecture is submitted for transcription, the system performs a duplicate check. If no existing transcript is found, a job containing the lecture\_id, audio\_url, and filename is published to the persistent RabbitMQ queue (transcription\_jobs). This decouples upload from processing and guarantees job persistence even if servers or workers restart.

The queue ensures:

1. FIFO processing  
2. Durable storage of jobs  
3. Resilience through retry and requeue mechanisms  
4. Ability to distribute workload across multiple worker nodes

#### **B. Worker Node — Whisper STT Processing** {#b.-worker-node-—-whisper-stt-processing}

Worker nodes continuously subscribe to the transcription\_jobs queue.  
For each job, the worker:

1. Validates again whether a transcript already exists (safety duplicate check).  
2. Downloads the complete audio file, handling server redirects.  
3. Processes the **entire audio file** using Whisper Turbo (no audio chunking).  
4. Receives segment-level timestamps directly from Whisper.  
5. Stores the raw transcript—along with its version metadata—into Supabase.

Once the raw transcript is saved, the system automatically publishes a new job to the **LLM correction queue**, ensuring a seamless transition into the next stage.

The worker acknowledges the job only after successful processing, ensuring no data loss during crashes or reboots.

#### **C. LLM Correction Pipeline (Automatically Queued After Transcription)** {#c.-llm-correction-pipeline-(automatically-queued-after-transcription)}

After a raw transcript is generated, the system automatically submits a correction job to a second RabbitMQ queue (llm\_correction\_jobs). A separate worker (LLM correction worker) processes these jobs.

For each correction job, the worker:

1. Fetches the latest raw transcript version from Supabase.  
2. Chunks transcript **text segments** (typically 20 segments per chunk).  
3. Sends each chunk to Gemini for linguistic cleanup, error correction, and normalization across Urdu, Arabic, and English.  
4. Merges corrected chunks into a unified transcript.  
5. Saves the corrected version as a **new version** linked to the same lecture.

This stage improves spelling consistency, enhances readability, and identifies low-confidence segments for later expert review in the Admin Panel.

#### **Pseudocode**  {#pseudocode}

| Algorithm TranscriptionPipelineInput: audio\_url, lecture\_idOutput: raw\_transcript\_id, corrected\_transcript\_id |
| :---- |

##### Figure 4.1.1 A: Transcription Queue {#figure-4.1.1-a:-transcription-queue}

| procedure SubmitTranscriptionJob(audio\_url, lecture\_id):    if check\_existing\_transcription(lecture\_id) exists:        return existing\_transcript\_id    job ← {        id: uuid(),        lecture\_id: lecture\_id,        audio\_url: audio\_url,        filename: extract\_filename(audio\_url)    }    RabbitMQ.enqueue("transcription\_jobs", job)    return job.id |
| :---- |

## 

##### Figure 4.1.1 B: Transcription Worker {#figure-4.1.1-b:-transcription-worker}

| procedure TranscriptionWorkerLoop():    while true:        job ← RabbitMQ.dequeue("transcription\_jobs")        try:            if check\_existing\_transcription(job.lecture\_id):                RabbitMQ.ack(job)                continue            audio\_file ← download\_audio(job.audio\_url)            raw\_segments ← Whisper.transcribe(audio\_file, language="ur")            transcript\_id ← SaveRawTranscript(job.lecture\_id, raw\_segments)            // Automatically enqueue correction job            SubmitCorrectionJob(transcript\_id)            RabbitMQ.ack(job)        catch error:            handle\_retry\_or\_fail(job) |
| :---- |

##### Figure 4.1.1 C: Correction Queue Submission {#figure-4.1.1-c:-correction-queue-submission}

| procedure SubmitCorrectionJob(transcript\_id):    job ← { id: uuid(), transcript\_id: transcript\_id }    RabbitMQ.enqueue("llm\_correction\_jobs", job) |
| :---- |

##### Figure 4.1.1 D: LLM Correction Worker {#figure-4.1.1-d:-llm-correction-worker}

| procedure CorrectionWorkerLoop():    while true:        job ← RabbitMQ.dequeue("llm\_correction\_jobs")        segments ← FetchTranscript(job.transcript\_id)        chunks ← SplitSegments(segments, size=20)        corrected ← \[\]        for chunk in chunks:            corrected\_chunk ← Gemini.correct(chunk)            corrected.extend(corrected\_chunk)        SaveCorrectedVersion(job.transcript\_id, corrected)        RabbitMQ.ack(job) |
| :---- |

### **4.1.2 Question Answering (Q\&A) with Citations — RAG Pipeline** {#4.1.2-question-answering-(q&a)-with-citations-—-rag-pipeline}

The RAG module provides citation-backed answers using verified transcript segments. The algorithm follows a standard retrieval-then-generation structure: 6,7

1. **Query Embedding:**  
   User query is embedded using the Gemini Embedding model (gemini-embedding-001).  
2. **Vector Search:**  
   The embedding is passed to Supabase Vector Store, retrieving the top-k semantically relevant transcript segments.  
3. **Reranking:**  
   Retrieved segments are filtered using a threshold to get rid of irrelevant ones and optionally reranked by similarity score.  
4. **Prompt Construction:**  
   A system prompt is built that includes only verified transcript text and metadata (lecture, timestamp).  
5. **Answer Generation (LLM via OpenRouter):**  
   The LLM generates an answer using **only** the retrieved evidence. Hallucination is mitigated by strict instructions and citation placeholders.  
6. **Citation Mapping:**  
   The model’s citation markers are mapped back to transcript segments and timestamps.

#### **RAG Retrieval** {#rag-retrieval}

##### Figure 4.1.2 A: RAG Retrieval Pipeline {#figure-4.1.2-a:-rag-retrieval-pipeline}

![][image11]

#### **RAG Ingestion** {#rag-ingestion}

##### Figure 4.1.2 B: RAG Ingestion Pipeline {#figure-4.1.2-b:-rag-ingestion-pipeline}

![][image12]

### **4.1.3 Knowledge Graph Construction (Entity Extraction, Normalization & Linking)** {#4.1.3-knowledge-graph-construction-(entity-extraction,-normalization-&-linking)}

The Knowledge Graph (KG) module structures Islamic learning content by identifying entities within lecture transcripts and linking them through semantically meaningful relationships. The goal of this pipeline is not to perform deep reasoning but to populate a graph database with canonical entities, allowing lectures, verses, hadith, and topics to be navigated and queried in a structured way.\[4\],\[5\]

The construction pipeline operates in three stages:

#### **A. Entity Extraction (LLM-Based)** {#a.-entity-extraction-(llm-based)}

Approved transcripts are processed using an LLM that extracts four categories of entities in a predefined JSON schema:

1. Ayah (text, surah, ayah number)  
2. Hadith (text, book, number, narrator)  
3. Topics (topic label, brief description)  
4. Headings/Subsections (title and span)

The extraction is chunk-based to handle long transcripts and ensure consistent output formatting. All extracted entities are merged and stored as structured objects.

#### **B. Normalization & Canonical Mapping** {#b.-normalization-&-canonical-mapping}

Extracted entities are then normalized:

* Ayat are aligned to canonical surah/ayah identifiers when similarity ≥ 90%.  
* Topic labels are mapped to existing categories when possible (e.g., namaz → salah, roza → sawm).  
* Duplicates are removed at a per-transcript level.

This ensures consistent identifiers before generating graph triples.

#### **C. Graph Population (TTL Generation)** {#c.-graph-population-(ttl-generation)}

After normalization, the system generates Turtle (TTL) RDF files representing the entities and their relationships. For each lecture, the following triple patterns are produced:

1. Lecture → mentions → Ayah/Hadith/Topic  
2. Lecture → hasTopic → Topic  
3. Series → hasLecture → Lecture  
4. Topic → discussedIn → Lecture  
5. Ayah/Hadith → mentionedIn → Lecture

These triples are exported as TTL files, enabling ingestion into any RDF-compatible triplestore. In this implementation, GraphDB was utilized.

### **4.1.4 Entity Verification & Consistency Enforcement** {#4.1.4-entity-verification-&-consistency-enforcement}

The entity extraction component processes approved lecture transcripts to identify Qur’anic verses (Ayat), Hadith references, thematic topics, and transcript headings. The system uses a Large Language Model (Gemini via OpenRouter) to extract entities in a structured JSON schema.

To handle long lectures, the pipeline processes transcripts in fixed-size text segments (chunks) and aggregates results before storing them in Supabase.

##### Figure 4.1.4: Entity Verification & Consistency Enforcement {#figure-4.1.4:-entity-verification-&-consistency-enforcement}

| Algorithm EntityExtractionPipelineInput: transcription\_idOutput: extracted\_entitiesprocedure ProcessEntityExtraction(transcription\_id):    // Fetch transcript text    segments ← FetchTranscriptFromSupabase(transcription\_id)    // Chunk transcript for processing    chunks ← SplitSegments(segments, CHUNK\_SIZE \= 100)    aggregated ← {        ayat: \[\],        hadith: \[\],        topics: \[\],        headings: \[\]    }    // Process each chunk with LLM    for chunk in chunks:        try:            response ← LLMExtractEntities(chunk)            aggregated.ayat.extend(response.ayat)            aggregated.hadith.extend(response.hadith)            aggregated.topics.extend(response.topics)            aggregated.headings.extend(response.headings)        catch error:            retry\_or\_log(chunk)    // Store aggregated results    SaveEntitiesToSupabase(transcription\_id, aggregated)// \--- LLM Extraction Call \---procedure LLMExtractEntities(chunk):    text ← FormatSegments(chunk)    response ← OpenRouter.call(        model="gemini-2.5-flash",        messages=\[            {role:"system", content: ENTITY\_EXTRACTION\_PROMPT},            {role:"user", content: text}        \],        response\_format=json\_schema(EntityExtractionSchema)    )    return ParseEntityExtraction(response) |
| :---- |

### 

### 

### **4.1.5 Automated Data Update Workflow (Versioning, Downloading & Integrity Validation)** {#4.1.5-automated-data-update-workflow-(versioning,-downloading-&-integrity-validation)}

The mobile and web application includes an automated data-update mechanism that ensures users always receive the latest database content without requiring an app update. The workflow runs at app launch and checks whether a new database version is available on the remote server. If a newer version exists, the system securely downloads, validates, and installs the updated database file.

#### **A. Remote Configuration Fetch** {#a.-remote-configuration-fetch}

At startup, the app retrieves a configuration file from a remote server containing:

1. the latest database version number,  
2. the download URL of the updated database file.

If the configuration cannot be fetched (e.g., network unavailable), the system simply proceeds with the locally stored database.

#### **B. Version Comparison** {#b.-version-comparison}

The system compares:

1. the local database version stored in SharedPreferences (local storage)  
2. the remote version from the configuration.

If the remote version is greater, the application initiates an update; otherwise, the app continues normally.

#### **C. Database Download & Installation** {#c.-database-download-&-installation}

When an update is required:

1. The app downloads a ZIP file containing the updated SQLite database.  
2. Any active database connections are closed to prevent file locking issues.  
3. The ZIP is extracted, and the .db file replaces the existing local database.  
4. An integrity check verifies that required tables exist before finalizing the update.

If verification fails, the system aborts the update and retains the previous database.

#### **D. Persisting the New Version** {#d.-persisting-the-new-version}

Once the update is successfully installed and validated:

1. the new version number is saved in SharedPreferences (local storage)  
2. temporary files are deleted  
3. the app resumes with the updated database

### **Pseudocode** {#pseudocode-1}

##### Table 4.1.5: Data Update Algorithm {#table-4.1.5:-data-update-algorithm}

| Algorithm DataUpdateWorkflowInput: NoneOutput: Updated local database (if newer version available)procedure InitializeApp():    config ← FetchRemoteConfig()    if config \= null:        return  // continue using local database    localVersion ← ReadLocalVersion()    remoteVersion ← config.database.version    if remoteVersion ≤ localVersion:        return  // no update needed    zipFile ← Download(config.database.url)    CloseDatabaseConnections()    newDb ← ExtractDatabase(zipFile)        if VerifyIntegrity(newDb) \= false:        AbortUpdate()        return    ReplaceLocalDatabase(newDb)    SaveVersion(remoteVersion)    CleanupTemporaryFiles() |
| :---- |

## 

### **4.1.6 Short-Form Content Generation Pipeline** {#4.1.6-short-form-content-generation-pipeline}

In FYP2, the short-form content module was extended from a planned feature into an automated pipeline. The purpose of this module is to convert long Islamic lectures into concise audio clips that can be understood independently. This was necessary because many Al-Huda lectures are one to two hours long, while mobile users often prefer shorter learning sessions. The system therefore needed a way to select meaningful sections without losing religious context.

The short-form pipeline uses already available transcript information instead of starting from raw audio only. It depends on two prerequisites: a corrected transcript version and extracted lecture headings. The corrected transcript provides clean segment text and timestamps, while the headings give topic boundaries. This allows the system to identify candidate clips from meaningful sections rather than cutting the lecture into arbitrary time windows.

The process begins when an admin or reviewer submits a short-content extraction job from the admin panel. The frontend checks that the backend is available, sends the selected transcription ID and maximum clip count, and then polls for job progress. On the backend, the FastAPI service validates that headings and a corrected transcript exist. If the prerequisites are satisfied, the job is stored in Redis and published to the RabbitMQ `short_content_jobs` queue. A dedicated worker then processes the job asynchronously, allowing long-running clip generation to happen without blocking the admin panel.

##### Figure 4.1.6: Short-Form Content Pipeline {#figure-4.1.6:-short-form-content-pipeline}

**[DIAGRAM PLACEHOLDER: Insert a sequence diagram showing Admin Panel → FastAPI → Redis → RabbitMQ → Short Content Worker → Gemini/OpenRouter → ffmpeg → Supabase Storage → Supabase Database. This is the most appropriate diagram here because the feature is an asynchronous pipeline with multiple services interacting in order.]**

The worker first fetches the lecture headings, corrected transcript segments, audio URL, and audio code from Supabase. It maps each transcript segment to a heading by checking the midpoint of the segment timestamp. This step groups transcript text under the heading where it belongs. Empty heading groups are ignored, and the remaining groups are packed into chunks that fit within the LLM context window.

For each chunk, the system sends the heading text and timestamped transcript segments to Gemini through OpenRouter. Gemini evaluates which parts of the lecture are suitable as standalone clips and returns structured output containing an English title, Urdu title, short description, independence score, and adjusted start and end times. The independence score is important because the clip must be understandable without requiring the listener to hear the entire lecture before it.

After all candidates are returned, the system filters and ranks them. Candidates with a low independence score are removed. Clips shorter than one minute or longer than twelve minutes are also removed. If two clips overlap, the higher-scored clip is kept. The final list is limited to the number of clips requested by the reviewer.

Once the final candidates are selected, the worker downloads the original lecture audio, slices the selected time ranges using ffmpeg, uploads the generated audio clips to Supabase Storage, and saves the metadata in Supabase. The saved metadata includes clip titles, descriptions, timestamps, duration, independence score, model name, generation time, and the public audio URL. This metadata is then displayed in the admin panel, where reviewers can play the clips and inspect their quality.

##### Table 4.1.6: Short-Form Content Algorithm {#table-4.1.6:-short-form-content-algorithm}

| Algorithm ShortFormContentGenerationInput: transcription\_id, max\_clipsOutput: extracted\_short\_contentprocedure GenerateShortContent(transcription\_id, max\_clips):    if headings do not exist or corrected version does not exist:        return validation error    job ← CreateJob(transcription\_id, max\_clips)    Redis.set(job.status, "pending")    RabbitMQ.enqueue("short\_content\_jobs", job)procedure ShortContentWorker(job):    data ← FetchHeadingsSegmentsAndAudio(job.transcription\_id)    heading\_groups ← MapSegmentsToHeadings(data.segments, data.headings)    chunks ← ChunkHeadingGroups(heading\_groups)    candidates ← \[\]    for chunk in chunks:        result ← Gemini.EvaluateStandaloneClips(chunk)        candidates.extend(result.clips)    selected ← FilterByScoreDurationAndOverlap(candidates, job.max\_clips)    audio\_file ← DownloadLectureAudio(data.audio\_url)    final\_clips ← \[\]    for clip in selected:        clip\_file ← FFmpeg.Slice(audio\_file, clip.start, clip.end)        clip\_url ← SupabaseStorage.Upload(clip\_file)        final\_clips.add(Metadata(clip, clip\_url))    SaveShortContent(job.transcription\_id, final\_clips)    Redis.set(job.status, "completed") |
| :---- |

### **4.1.7 Expert Flagging and Metrics Workflow** {#4.1.7-expert-flagging-and-metrics-workflow}

The expert review process is central to HudaAI because Islamic educational content must be accurate, contextual, and trustworthy. In FYP1, the admin panel allowed reviewers to edit and approve transcripts, but the review process still depended heavily on manual checking. In FYP2, a structured flagging workflow was added so that reviewers and experts can mark exact transcript segments that need attention.

The reason for adding flags was to make quality assurance more measurable. A transcript can contain different types of issues, such as timing mismatch, transcription error, unclear wording, missing Arabic text, or a correction that requires domain expertise. If these problems are only fixed informally, the team cannot easily track how many issues remain or which categories occur most often. Segment-level flags solve this by attaching each issue to a specific part of the transcript.

The workflow is divided into two review passes. In the first pass, the reviewer reads the transcript while listening to the audio and adds flags to problematic segments. Each flag is assigned a category and may include a note. In the second pass, the reviewer or expert fixes the flagged segments. Some categories can be fixed with AI assistance, while sensitive or context-heavy categories remain manual. This keeps the system efficient without removing expert control.

##### Figure 4.1.7: Expert Flagging and Metrics Workflow {#figure-4.1.7:-expert-flagging-and-metrics-workflow}

**[DIAGRAM PLACEHOLDER: Insert an activity diagram showing Review Transcript → Add Flag → Select Category → Save Version → Carry Unresolved Flags Forward → Fix Flagged Segments → Resolve Flags → Approve Transcript. This is the most appropriate diagram here because the feature is a human review workflow with decision points.]**

A key technical issue solved in FYP2 was flag continuity across transcript versions. When a reviewer saves changes, the system creates a new transcript version. If flags are attached only to segment positions, they can disappear or point to the wrong segment after edits, insertions, or deletions. To prevent this, each transcript segment now has a stable segment ID. Flags are stored with both the version information and the segment ID. When a new version is saved, unresolved flags are copied forward only if the same segment still exists in the new version. This ensures that remaining issues stay visible even after partial corrections.

The admin panel also includes a flag category management page. Admins can create categories, assign colors, add descriptions, and mark whether a category can be fixed using AI. If duplicate categories are created during review, the system allows merging categories so that old flags are preserved but grouped under a cleaner category structure.

Metrics were added alongside the flagging workflow to give reviewers a quick view of transcript quality. The transcript page shows the total number of segments, current version, approval status, open flags, resolved flags, flagged segment count, and category breakdown. The dashboard also shows high-level operational metrics such as pending reviews, approved files, total files, and generated short clips.

##### Table 4.1.7: Review Metrics Captured in Admin Panel {#table-4.1.7:-review-metrics-captured-in-admin-panel}

| Metric | Purpose | How it supports quality assurance |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Total segments | Shows the size of a transcript | Helps reviewers estimate review effort |
| Current version | Shows which transcript version is being reviewed | Maintains traceability after edits |
| Open flags | Counts unresolved issues | Shows remaining review work |
| Resolved flags | Counts fixed issues | Shows review progress |
| Flagged segments | Counts how many segments required attention | Indicates transcript quality |
| Category breakdown | Groups issues by type | Helps identify common pipeline weaknesses |
| Short clip count | Counts generated short-form clips | Shows output produced by the short-content pipeline |

## **4.2 External APIs/SDKs** {#4.2-external-apis/sdks}

This project integrates several third-party APIs and SDKs to support core functionalities such as speech-to-text transcription, large-language-model processing, workflow automation, vector search, caching, and message queue management. These external services form the backbone of the RAG pipeline, transcription pipeline, entity extraction workflow, and mobile-side interactions.

The following table provides a detailed overview of all external APIs/SDKs used in the implementation, including their purpose and the exact endpoint/function/class invoked by the system.

##### Table 4.2: External APIs/SDKs {#table-4.2:-external-apis/sdks}

| API and Version / Provider | Description | Purpose of Usage | API Endpoint / Function / Class Used |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Supabase (v2 Platform Services)** | Hosted PostgreSQL database \+ Storage \+ Auth | Stores lectures, transcripts, transcript versions, extracted entities, and job metadata | supabase.from('table').select()/insert()/update()(Dart & Node clients) |
| **OpenAI Whisper (Whisper-Turbo via API & Local Inference)** | Speech-to-Text model for Urdu/Arabic/English | Transcribes full audio files into timestamped segments | model.transcribe(audio\_filepath, language="ur") |
| **OpenRouter API (2024-xx) – LLM Gateway** | Unified gateway to multiple LLMs | Sends entity extraction and correction prompts to LLMs | POST https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions |
| **Google Gemini / GenAI (via OpenRouter)** | LLM used for transcript correction and entity extraction | Cleans transcripts, normalizes segments, extracts Ayat, Hadith, topics, headings | Model: "google/gemini-2.5-flash" |
| **Supabase Vector Store (v1.x)** | Vector database for semantic search | Stores transcript embeddings and performs top-k semantic retrieval for Q\&A | client.search(collection, query\_vector, limit) |
| **Redis (v7.x)** | In-memory caching engine | Caches transcription status, job metadata, and rate-limited operations for faster lookups | redis.get/set, redis.hget/hset |
| **RabbitMQ (v3.x)** | Message Queue for asynchronous job processing | Queues transcription, correction, entity extraction, heading extraction, RAG ingestion, and short-content jobs for worker-based processing | Queues: "transcription\_jobs", "llm\_correction\_jobs", "short\_content\_jobs" |
| **FFmpeg** | Audio processing utility | Cuts selected lecture time ranges into standalone audio clips without re-encoding | ffmpeg -ss \<start\> -to \<end\> -c copy |
| **n8n Workflow Automation (Self-Hosted, 2024 build)** | No-code workflow engine | Flutter app calls this webhook for RAG query → vector search → LLM answer generation | POST /webhook/\<workflow-id\> |

## **4.3 Testing Details** {#4.3-testing-details}

Testing was performed to verify that the system works as intended and that the features added in FYP2 support the requirements of reliable Islamic content delivery. Since HudaAI combines AI-generated output with expert-reviewed educational material, testing was not limited to code execution. It included automated tests, integration checks, manual testing by the project team, internal validation by the Al-Huda team, and user acceptance testing with beta users.

The testing approach was designed around two goals. The first goal was to verify that individual software components work correctly, such as clip filtering, API response handling, queue submission, and worker processing. The second goal was to validate whether the generated content is actually useful, accurate, and understandable for the target users. This second goal required expert review and real user feedback, because automated tests cannot fully judge the correctness of Islamic terminology, Urdu/Arabic phrasing, or whether a short clip preserves the intended context.

### **4.3.1 Unit Testing** {#4.3.1-unit-testing}

Each unit test targets a single function or method to validate correctness in isolation. The automated tests cover the transcription pipeline, RAG pipeline, entity extraction, short-form content generation, AI route behavior, uploaded segment validation, and frontend review components.

In the backend pipeline, the short-form content tests verify that transcript segments are mapped to headings correctly, heading groups are chunked without splitting a section, weak clip candidates are filtered out, overlapping clips are handled, and the maximum clip count is enforced. This is important because the quality of a generated clip depends on both semantic boundaries and timing boundaries.

In the admin panel, unit and component tests verify frontend behavior such as segment validation, AI segment actions, download formatting, transcription job status handling, and segment row interaction. These tests reduce the risk of breaking the reviewer workflow while adding new expert review features.

##### Table 4.3.1: Unit Testing Table {#table-4.3.1:-unit-testing-table}

| Test case ID | Test Objective | Precondition | Steps | Test Data | Expected Result | Post-condition | Actual Result | Pass/Fail |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **TC001** | Verify audio transcription using Whisper | Audio file exists; Whisper model loaded | 1\. Call transcribe\_audio() 2\. Inspect returned segments | sample\_urdu\_lecture.mp3(30s) | Returns list of segments with start/end/text | Segments available in memory | As Expected | Pass |
| **TC002** | Validate entity extraction from transcript chunk | Gemini/OpenRouter API reachable | 1\. Call extract\_entities\_from\_chunk() 2\. Inspect structured output | Chunk with ayah, hadith references | Entities returned in correct JSON schema | Entities stored in object | As Expected | Pass |
| **TC003** | Verify embedding generation | Gemini Embeddings API reachable | 1\. Call embed\_query() 2\. Inspect vector | “Prayer is pillar of religion…” | Returns 768-dim embedding vector | Vector ready for Supabase vector db | As Expected | Pass |
| **TC004** | Validate RAG prompt construction | Retrieved segments available | 1\. Call build\_rag\_prompt() 2\. Verify citations | Query: “Ruling on fasting” | Prompt includes citations \+ metadata | Prompt ready for LLM | As Expected | Pass |
| **TC005** | Verify audio download from URL | Audio URL accessible | 1\. Call download\_audio() 2\. Check local file | https://.../Media/lecture.mp3 | File downloaded and readable | File saved to temp directory | As Expected | Pass |
| **TC006** | Validate Supabase transcription save | Supabase connection active | 1\. Call save\_to\_supabase() 2\. Query Supabase | 10 segments \+ lecture\_id=123 | Returns transcript\_id \+ version\_id | Record stored in DB | As Expected | Pass |
| **TC007** | Verify Redis job status update | Redis connection active | 1\. Call update\_job\_status\_in\_redis() 2\. Fetch value | Job ID: job-123 | Redis key updated with status | Key exists in Redis | As Expected | Pass |
| **TC008** | Validate 90%+ similarity ayah matching | Quran DB loaded; similarity func available | 1\. Call ayah matching fn 2\. Inspect match | Extracted ayah text | Returns surah 2:78 with ≥90% similarity | Entity marked verified | As Expected | Pass |
| **TC009** | Verify transcription job queue processing | RabbitMQ active; worker running | 1\. Publish job 2\. Worker processes 3\. Check Redis status | {"job\_id": "test-001", "lecture\_id": 123} | Job moves pending→processing→done | Transcript saved | As Expected | Pass |
| **TC010** | Validate LLM correction pipeline | LLM correction queue active | 1\. Call correct\_segments\_with\_llm() 2\. Inspect output | 20 segments Urdu text | Returns corrected segments | Corrected version generated | As Expected | Pass |
| **TC011** | Verify Supabase semantic search | Supabase vector store collection exists | 1\. embed query 2\. supabase.search() | Query: “Importance of prayer” | Returns top-k results with score\>0.7 | Segments ready for RAG | As Expected | Pass |
| **TC012** | Validate segment conversion | Whisper output available | 1\. Call convert\_segments\_to\_lecture\_segments() | raw segments: start/end/text | Returns valid Pydantic LectureSegment list | Segments ready for DB | As Expected | Pass |
| **TC013** | Validate segment-to-heading mapping for short clips | Headings and transcript segments available | 1\. Calculate segment midpoint 2\. Assign segment to heading | Two headings and timestamped segments | Segment is placed under correct heading | Heading group created | As Expected | Pass |
| **TC014** | Validate short-content clip filtering | Candidate clips available | 1\. Filter by score 2\. Filter by duration 3\. Remove overlaps | Candidates with mixed scores and durations | Only valid standalone clips remain | Ranked clip list created | As Expected | Pass |
| **TC015** | Verify flag creation on transcript segment | User authenticated; flag category exists | 1\. Select segment 2\. Add flag category 3\. Save flag | Segment ID and category ID | Flag saved against segment | Flag appears in reviewer view | As Expected | Pass |
| **TC016** | Verify unresolved flag continuity after saving | Transcript has unresolved flags | 1\. Save edited transcript 2\. Open new version | Flagged segment with stable ID | Unresolved flag remains visible in new version | Reviewer continues remaining fixes | As Expected | Pass |

### **4.3.2 Integration and API Testing** {#4.3.2-integration-and-api-testing}

Integration testing was used to verify that separate parts of the system communicate correctly. This was especially important because the backend uses asynchronous processing: the API accepts a job, RabbitMQ dispatches it, the worker processes it, Redis stores progress, and Supabase stores the final result.

For the short-form content pipeline, API tests verify that the system does not queue a job unless the transcript has extracted headings and a corrected version. This prevents invalid processing and gives the reviewer immediate feedback. Worker callback tests verify that valid messages are acknowledged, invalid JSON is rejected, missing fields are rejected, and failed jobs are retried or discarded according to the retry policy.

The admin panel also includes API route tests for AI-assisted segment fixing and translation. These tests check timeout handling, fallback behavior when one API key fails, and item-level failure isolation in batch requests. This is necessary because the review system should not fail completely if one AI request fails.

##### Table 4.3.2: Testing Strategy Summary {#table-4.3.2:-testing-strategy-summary}

| Testing Type | Area Tested | Purpose | Result |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Unit testing | Short-content helper functions | Validate mapping, chunking, filtering, ranking, and overlap removal | Core algorithmic logic verified |
| API testing | FastAPI queue endpoints | Ensure valid jobs are queued and invalid jobs are rejected | Queue submission behavior verified |
| Worker testing | RabbitMQ callbacks | Ensure workers acknowledge, reject, retry, or discard messages correctly | Worker reliability verified |
| Frontend component testing | Segment list and review interactions | Verify reviewer-facing controls behave correctly | Review UI behavior verified |
| Service testing | Supabase, Redis, and job service wrappers | Confirm data flow and status handling | Service behavior verified through mocks |
| Manual testing | Admin panel and generated outputs | Confirm real workflows are usable end to end | Issues identified and corrected during development |

##### Figure 4.3.1: Testing and QA Layers {#figure-4.3.1:-testing-and-qa-layers}

**[DIAGRAM PLACEHOLDER: Insert a layered testing diagram with Unit Tests at the bottom, API/Integration Tests above it, Worker/Queue Tests above that, Internal Al-Huda Expert Review above that, and Beta User Acceptance Testing at the top. This is the most appropriate diagram here because the project combines software correctness testing with human validation.]**

### **4.3.3 User Acceptance and Expert Validation** {#4.3.3-user-acceptance-and-expert-validation}

User acceptance testing was performed with more than 3000 beta testers through Apple TestFlight and Google Play testing channels. This testing validated the user-facing experience of HudaAI on real mobile devices. It helped confirm that users could browse lectures, play audio, interact with transcripts, and use the application outside a controlled development environment.

Internal testing was also performed with the Al-Huda team, including reviewers and experts. This validation was necessary because the project deals with Islamic educational content, where correctness cannot be judged by software tests alone. Experts reviewed transcripts, checked segment quality, used the flagging workflow, and evaluated whether generated short clips were understandable and contextually safe.

The project team also performed self-testing throughout development. This included submitting jobs from the admin panel, checking job polling, reviewing generated clips, playing sliced audio, adding and resolving flags, and confirming that unresolved flags remain visible after saving a new transcript version. These checks helped ensure that the implemented workflow matched the actual review process required by the Al-Huda team.

For further quality assurance, a team of experts was onboarded into the system. This creates a scalable human-in-the-loop review process. Instead of relying only on developers to inspect generated content, the system allows qualified reviewers to identify issues, categorize them, resolve them, and provide structured feedback through flags and metrics.

Overall, the testing process shows that HudaAI was evaluated at three levels: software correctness, workflow integration, and real-world content validation. This is important because the system is not only a technical pipeline; it is a platform for delivering verified Islamic learning content.
